header.subtitle: Module 10: TDD with AI | Tests as Contract
sec.paradoxo.intro: Asking AI to "generate tests for this function" produces tests that validate what the code does, not what the code should do. Coverage climbs fast, confidence doesn't follow. The paradox lies in using the wrong metric for the right question.
sec.paradoxo.card.coverage.title: Coverage ≠ Confidence
sec.paradoxo.card.coverage.desc: 95% coverage with empty mocks and tautological assertions. The report goes green. The production bug is still there. Coverage measures lines executed, not behavior guaranteed.
sec.paradoxo.card.spec.title: Tests as Spec
sec.paradoxo.card.spec.desc: 60% coverage with tests that fail when someone breaks the contract. Each test is a sentence of the contract. If the sentence is red, some promise was broken.
sec.paradoxo.toggle.title: Same function, two test styles:
sec.paradoxo.chart.title: Coverage rises, regressions persist
sec.paradoxo.chart.caption: Illustrative values for discussion, not a benchmark.
sec.inversao.intro: The classic red-green-refactor cycle assumes a human writes the test. When AI enters the equation, the order matters much more. Whoever writes the test first defines the contract. Whoever writes the code first defines what gets tested.
sec.inversao.col.classic.label: Traditional
sec.inversao.col.classic.note: Works, but it's slow. Good for critical projects.
sec.inversao.col.bad.label: AI generating test after code
sec.inversao.col.bad.li.3: Coverage rises, value stays at zero
sec.inversao.col.bad.note: Pure test theater: validation that validates itself.
sec.inversao.col.good.label: Recommended
sec.inversao.col.good.li.1: Human writes the red test (defines the contract)
sec.inversao.col.good.li.2: AI implements to make the tests pass
sec.inversao.col.good.note: AI's speed, the human contract's guarantee.
sec.inversao.tabs.title: Three prompt levels for generating tests:
sec.inversao.prompt.bad.note: AI invents scenarios based on what it sees in the code. It has no way of knowing the real contract.
sec.inversao.prompt.ok.note: Better than the previous one, but still only covers what AI can see. No explicit contract.
sec.inversao.prompt.good.note: Explicit contract. AI can't invent its way to a passing test. It has to actually implement.
sec.pipeline.intro: Tests don't live alone. They take part in the integrated system from the previous session. Hooks run tests, agents fix the code when they fail, memories store patterns. The result is a cycle where AI proposes, the test validates, the human approves.
sec.pipeline.step.1.title: AI writes code
sec.pipeline.step.1.desc: Write or Edit tools change a project file
sec.pipeline.step.2.title: PostToolUse hook runs the tests
sec.pipeline.step.2.desc: Bash script picks the right runner based on the file name
sec.pipeline.step.3.title: Tests fail and return stderr
sec.pipeline.step.3.desc: Exit code 1 and the runner's message are read by Claude
sec.pipeline.step.4.title: Agent fixes the code (not the test)
sec.pipeline.step.4.desc: The rule is hard: the contract defined in the test is immutable
sec.pipeline.step.5.title: Hook runs again, exit 0
sec.pipeline.step.5.desc: Memory stores the pattern to avoid the same mistake later
sec.pipeline.code.settings.title: Hook config in .claude/settings.json
sec.pipeline.code.script.title: Script .claude/hooks/run-tests.sh
sec.pipeline.code.memory.title: Feedback memory in .claude/memory/feedback_tests.md
sec.pipeline.insight.title: The unbreakable rule
sec.pipeline.insight.desc: When a test fails, the agent fixes the code, never the test. If the test is actually wrong, the human changes it in the next commit. Mixing those two situations creates the worst of both worlds: tests that bend to the bug.
sec.mocks.intro: Mocking is an architectural decision, not a default pattern. Each level of the test pyramid pays a different price in speed and gains a different value in guarantees. The problem with "mock everything" is that speed turns into an illusion of coverage.
sec.mocks.pyramid.contract.title: Contract test (more real, more value)
sec.mocks.pyramid.contract.desc: Validates that the real API fulfills the shared contract between services
sec.mocks.pyramid.integration.title: Integration test
sec.mocks.pyramid.integration.desc: Real Postgres via testcontainers, validates the actual query
sec.mocks.pyramid.unit.title: Unit test (cheapest, least value)
sec.mocks.pyramid.unit.desc: Mocks everything. Only makes sense for pure logic without I/O
sec.mocks.table.unit.never: When SQL, HTTP, or filesystem are involved
sec.mocks.table.integration.never: When the problem is purely logic
sec.mocks.warning.title: The pyramid inversion
sec.mocks.warning.desc: Teams that ask AI to "generate tests" usually get 95% unit tests with mocks. The pyramid inverts: lots of mocks, little real coverage. The result is false confidence. Force the discussion about which level each test belongs to before generating a single line.
sec.antipatterns.intro: Four traps appear often when AI writes tests without contract supervision. Spotting them before code review prevents institutional test theater.
sec.antipatterns.card.1.title: Tautology
sec.antipatterns.card.1.wrong.label: Wrong
sec.antipatterns.card.1.wrong.note: Proves nothing. It exists only to inflate coverage.
sec.antipatterns.card.1.right.label: Right
sec.antipatterns.card.2.title: Mock-everything
sec.antipatterns.card.2.wrong.label: Wrong
sec.antipatterns.card.2.wrong.note: You're testing the mock, not the code.
sec.antipatterns.card.2.right.label: Right
sec.antipatterns.card.2.right.note: Mock external I/O only when truly testing it isn't viable.
sec.antipatterns.card.3.title: Snapshot-only
sec.antipatterns.card.3.wrong.label: Wrong
sec.antipatterns.card.3.wrong.note: Snapshot breaks on every CSS change. Diff gets approved without review.
sec.antipatterns.card.3.right.label: Right
sec.antipatterns.card.3.right.note: Semantic assertion, robust to visual changes.
sec.antipatterns.card.4.title: Happy-path only
sec.antipatterns.card.4.wrong.label: Wrong
sec.antipatterns.card.4.wrong.note: One test per function, always with valid input. Edge cases stay uncovered.
sec.antipatterns.card.4.right.label: Right
sec.antipatterns.card.4.right.note: Explicit table of edge cases, easy to extend.
sec.metricas.intro: Coverage is an implementation metric: how many lines the test touched. Bugs prevented is a product metric: how many defects reached the user. The two numbers are not correlated. Pick which one you want to optimize before asking AI to write a single line of test.
sec.metricas.chart.caption: Illustrative values for discussion, not a real benchmark.
sec.metricas.card.1.title: Time-to-bug-in-prod
sec.metricas.card.2.title: Refactor confidence
sec.metricas.card.3.title: Bugs caught before merge
sec.metricas.card.3.desc: CI runs the contracts, AI fixes
sec.metricas.insight.title: Coverage is process. Bugs prevented is outcome.
sec.metricas.insight.desc: Coverage measures how much the test wandered through the code's lines. Bugs prevented measures how much the user was protected. The two are not correlated. Pick the second one as the discussion metric in reviews and reports.
sec.cucumber.intro: When the contract has to be understood by people who don't write code (PM, designer, QA, product stakeholder), Gherkin is a pragmatic choice. The .feature file becomes the contract: natural language describing behavior. AI reads that contract and generates both the implementation and the step definitions, but the source of truth lives in a file anyone on the team can review.
sec.cucumber.why.title: Why consider BDD with Cucumber
sec.cucumber.why.li.1: PM, designer, and QA can read and contribute scenarios without opening the IDE
sec.cucumber.why.li.2: The spec is versioned alongside the code, on the same branch and in the same PR
sec.cucumber.why.li.3: AI receives a contract in natural language and generates implementation + step definitions
sec.cucumber.why.li.4: Reduces ambiguity: discussions between tech and product happen on top of the same file
sec.cucumber.why.li.5: Scenarios serve as living documentation, always aligned with what the system actually does
sec.cucumber.feature.title: Example: features/parse_date.feature
sec.cucumber.feature.note: This file is the contract. PM, QA, and dev read and review the same source of truth.
sec.cucumber.steps.title: Step definitions generated by AI: features/steps/parse_date.steps.js
sec.cucumber.steps.note: AI generates the glue code from the .feature. You review the assertions, not the syntax.
sec.cucumber.tradeoff.pros.title: When it's worth it
sec.cucumber.tradeoff.pros.li.1: Product defines business rules that change frequently
sec.cucumber.tradeoff.pros.li.2: QA participates in writing scenarios, not just running them
sec.cucumber.tradeoff.pros.li.3: Compliance, regulation, or audit needs to read the spec
sec.cucumber.tradeoff.pros.li.4: Distributed teams where async alignment is critical
sec.cucumber.tradeoff.cons.title: When the cost outweighs the gain
sec.cucumber.tradeoff.cons.li.1: Purely technical logic with no non-tech stakeholder involved
sec.cucumber.tradeoff.cons.li.2: Small team where dev and PM are the same person
sec.cucumber.tradeoff.cons.li.3: Step definitions becoming a regex salad that's hard to maintain
sec.cucumber.tradeoff.cons.li.4: Scenarios turning into field dumps instead of real behavior
sec.cucumber.setup.title: Minimum setup (Node.js)
sec.cucumber.setup.note: For Python use behave, for Ruby use cucumber-ruby, for Java use cucumber-jvm. The idea is the same.
sec.cucumber.insight.title: The contract lives outside the code
sec.cucumber.insight.desc: The big advantage isn't Cucumber itself, it's where the contract lives. When it's in Gherkin, it leaves the dev silo and becomes a shared artifact. PM reviews, QA expands, dev implements, AI generates glue. The source of truth stops being the code and becomes the described behavior.
sec.proximos.intro: Four concrete moves to start treating tests as contracts in your project.
sec.proximos.step.1.title: Start with the contract
sec.proximos.step.1.desc: Before asking AI for code, write the 3 to 5 most important edge-case scenarios as tests. That's the contract AI has to fulfill.
sec.proximos.step.2.title: Set up the test hook
sec.proximos.step.2.desc: Create .claude/hooks/run-tests.sh and register it as PostToolUse for Write|Edit in .claude/settings.json. The cycle starts running on its own.
sec.proximos.step.3.title: Save patterns to memory
sec.proximos.step.3.desc: When the team decides "all auth tests use testcontainers", save it as feedback_tests.md in .claude/memory/. It becomes an automatic rule for the next sessions.
sec.proximos.step.4.title: Audit the existing test suite
sec.proximos.step.4.desc: Use an agent to look for tautologies, mock-everything, and snapshot-only in your repo. The result is usually painful but necessary.
sec.proximos.mantra.label: Part 10 Mantra:
sec.proximos.mantra.text: "Tests don't prove the code works. They specify what it should do."
